speaker: interviewer
text: A-N-C-- Flancbaum. I'm sorry. F-L-A-N-C-B-A-U-M. And this is roll number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to take you back a little bit, while you were in the ghetto, did you have any news of what was going on in the outside world beyond the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we had news, but not too much. We had a radio. In our town maybe was three radios. The whole town had three radios. And it was very, very hard to-- to communicate with the people. In the-- in the-- in the, ghetto we were not allowed to have a radio. Just one radio, I believe, was like an underground. Until the news came to me was already-- I didn't know too much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you receive any letters or packages from anyone outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? In the--

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of anyone who might have tried to escape from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I escaped from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, OK. Let's just go back to October of 1942, and you were telling us that you were going to another place. You were leaving the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we went-- the-- I went to work in [INAUDIBLE]. I worked there a long, a long-- a pretty long time. And one time, when I was there in the ghetto, we were talking to friends already. We tried to organize ourself into-- into escape. While we were in the ghetto, working in that-- ammunition factory because we-- we-- we saw that over there is-- the end is gonna-- they're-- they're gonna kill me. Then I said, I-- and another few fellows like me, we said, to be here is for sure death. Let's try to do something different.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then one said, [INAUDIBLE], what you gonna do? What? You have no ammunition. You have nothing. I said, look. I said, [INAUDIBLE], this is no place to be because we're gonna get killed here. It's no use to be here. We have to do something. I was dreaming and dreaming, and I really didn't know what I want-- what I want to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my brother, especially my brother. My brother encouraged me always, think. Think to escape. The minute he came into the-- to that place, it-- it was very hard work. Very difficult work while we-- while we were working. Let's say we had shifts. We used to work from 8:00 to 5:00, and from 5:00 to 12:00 and from 12:00 to 6:00. If-- if-- when we came on the last shift, it was very tiring. Winter was cold. Very bad. One day I fell asleep while I was working. I fell-- I was tired. I fell asleep. Then a guy came with a cold-- how do you call it-- a-- pail of water and throw at me. And I came up, and I said, oy. I-- I got scared because I shouldn't do that. And I kept trying to work, soaking wet. A shirt, I had no shirt. I had only a shirt with-- the-- the-- the shirt was only this piece was left, and the arm sleeve [? tore this ?] piece from the-- excuse me. Excuse my expression, from lice. They eat us out. The lice was eating us up alive. From such a bad condition. We-- we washed ourselves very little because we had no soap. The condition was impossible. Unbelievable. No humanly. Nothing. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like? Did you live in a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a barrack, yes. Yeah, I used-- we-- we used to sleep three stories, three-story barracks. It was maybe about 150 people in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to share your bunk with somebody? Another man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure. Look, we were sleeping, let's say, like-- like maybe 30 fellows on-- on one line, or maybe more. I don't remember even. And yes, I got sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got sick of typhus. That was in winter time. I used to get out outside. I had a big-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you say, up top? A big dish, and I used to get water in that dish and drink it out. In the morning, in the morning used to-- to used to come a guy, a Jewish fellow. And he used to give every-- and we were in a barrack, all the sick people used to be in one place. All the sick people, and I was between those, then he used to bring the bread. He used to carry it on a tray, and I was on the top. I took maybe five or six. I shouldn't take it, but I was hungry. I took it. A lot of people couldn't eat, and I ate like-- like a horse. I could eat like a horse. A lot of people couldn't eat at all. And I was eating. Then all of a sudden, the guy asked himself, where in the hell, who takes that bread? Who takes away all the portions of bread, because he knew he didn't give up nobody. I ate a lot of bread, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later I got well, after a little time. No medicine. For myself, I got well. I got well. I went back to work. I-- I said, I don't want to be in this barrack, because this barrack was-- if they're gonna come one day, they're going to shoot everyone out. Because they-- they kill all the people. Whoever was sick, they kill them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe this was a week after-- after, maybe 10 days. I don't remember how long, and I went back to my barrack. In my barrack, I still wasn't well. Because at night, I used to sing. Why was I singing? I had a temperature. I didn't know that I have temperature. Then the next pit said, Hyman, why don't you stop it? And I didn't know that I'm doing it, because my fever was so great. It was like a 100-- like-- like had a 42 temperature. Here it's maybe 104. I don't know how much that is. Then the policeman-- we had-- in each barrack had a policeman. And like a-- like a-- how do you call it? Like a manager. He said to me, if you will not behave, I'm gonna switch you from this barrack to the next barrack. I said, [? Ralph, ?] I-- I don't do it because I want it. I do it. I am singing. And I had to tell him that I got-- I have a temperature. I trust him. And the next day, they took me out from this place. They took me back to the other barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there another few days. I was drinking a lot of cold water, and that helped me to lower down the temperature. Maybe I was there in this place another 10 or 12 days. I don't remember how long. And I recovered. And I went back to work. When I went back to work, this was almost before Christmas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was before Christmas. And Christmas, they used-- they give us 10 cigarettes, each have-- each guy, each man, and a little bottle of vodka. I didn't drink, and I didn't smoke. And we had a boss-- terrible guy-- that I knew if he-- every day he had somebody like for breakfast, he killed somebody. Not to kill them, but to beat them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I said to myself, maybe next, I'll be next. And I walked over to this guy when I got those cigarettes and the bottle of vodka. This was a day before Christmas. And I said, you know what? I don't drink and I don't smoke, and I would like to give you a present.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, he didn't-- he didn't accept it right away. I said, please. Please take it from me, because I have no use for that. Then he answered me, sell it. You'll get yourself a piece of it. I said, I would rather give it to you. Oh sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, we came to work. We always had to like-- we have to-- my name Hyman Flancbaum. Yes. That and this, they called up our names. And when he called my name, he called me, step forward and come out. I thought, god forbid, what's gonna? He said, today, from now on, you're gonna work in this and this department. I will not have to work outside. Just inside. And this was a blessing. This was just like-- like I was promoted to the highest job. What work did you do outside, before you got the promotion?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were making roads. I was good in this, because I worked, before I came to this place, I used to work on roads. As I told you, right from-- from the beginning, from 1939. Then when I worked there, then he-- then one day came a big transport of [INAUDIBLE]. They-- they made-- how do you call that? They cleaned up a town, a Jewish town, and they brought a lot of Jewish things, beautiful things. All kinds of good stuff. And I picked-- I knew what to pick because I wasn't dumb. I was a smart kid. And I said, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], this is gonna be good for your wife. She's can hang this up on the wall, a [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't know what the hell this is. I said, you're gonna tell your wife to hang it up and it's gonna be beautiful. And he liked me for that. He liked me for that. And I worked there. While I was working there, I got in contact with Polish people. And we-- we were six or eight fellows. We get organized ourselves, and we said, one day we're going to escape from there. And in the-- in the-- in the concentration-- in the workplace, we had already-- we had communication with the Polacks, how the Russian are advancing. At that time, we had already a little bit of what's happening in the world. Politics. How-- how the Germans are strong, and how the Russians are fighting them. And in England, England stepped in to the war. The United States stepped in to the war-- I mean, to the war already. Then we knew. Then we said, one day you're gonna escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the work camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you observe any of the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know Jewish holidays. We didn't know nothing. We had no connection, no. Nothing. Zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any selections made while you were in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every, practically every week was a selection. 10 people killed. 20 people, 30 people killed. 40 people killed. It was-- I forgot to tell you, when I was in that barrack, one day-- this I forgot to tell you. It is very important. Our barrack was isolated because we all had typhus. There was maybe about 51 to 55 people in that barrack. And everyone was shot. And I was there, too. And I was on the first floor, laying on the first floor. When I was on the first floor, after they shoot, after everybody was shot, I came out, and I recognized the guy. And I said, look, I am not-- I am alive. Please, let-- take me out from here because the-- the SS was there right away, and the Jews had to bury them. And I escaped. I and another kid escaped from that barrack. And the other was in  Israel, and I am here alive. This I forgot to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you actually see these people being shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually see these people being shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them. Most of the people were shot. I-- I-- I-- I didn't have too  much time to look because I wanted to save myself. I right away-- later I found out that I and another kid, we remained at this-- this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the names of any of the people who did the killing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I know. [? Altov ?] was his name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ukrainian people was killed. They-- I paid off these Ukrainian people which he killed. Those in my barrack, the people, I paid him off because when I was working for this-- for this, guy, for this [INAUDIBLE] which I told you, the guy was-- he used to drink. The night, while I was-- while I was picking so many good things, I took a few pieces and I-- I came hope to the barrack, and I sold it to a shirt or a pair of pants. And I got for this a few dollars. What did I need money? I didn't need money to save. I bought a bottle of whiskey, of vodka, to buy that Polack. To get him that-- my-- my-- my-- my supervisor was a Polack, a Volksdeutsche. And he needed to drink. And I had to feed him, his habit. That I used to give him every day. And this guy knew. I mean, this-- this Ukrainian, which he killed those people, he was taking place, he was-- he actually killed a lot of people. A few of them. And he knew me and I knew him, because I used to give him money. Him I-- he-- he-- he I didn't get for like Vodka. I give him money to let me go through because he knew that I am-- I have full [INAUDIBLE] on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. The food was-- they give us 20 gram of-- of bread a day. And and soup. Everyone got the soup. If somebody was lucky, he got two potatoes. Or one potato. Or it'd be plain water. That was twice. We got in the-- in the morning, we got a piece of bread. And-- and like, before 4 o'clock, we got to soup. That was the food. Terrible. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the camp anywhere near a town, or was it very isolated, that you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was isolated. True.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the sanitary conditions of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no-- nothing. Zero. No soap. Not enough water to-- not enough water, plain water, to wash ourselves. The place where we-- we-- never was cleaned. Never. The toilets was terrible. It was outside the place. And a matter of fact, those toilets are used when I escaped. When-- when I escaped, this was in 1944. I'm going so fast. In 1944, I-- now yes-- my brother knew the area very, very well, where we used-- where we lived. And we had a place-- we-- we-- we said to ourselves, you're going to go to this and this place. And my brother knew this area because he used to deal with the-- with the country people. He used to know them. Because he-- and the-- and the-- in the [? war, ?] he used to-- his business was like buying [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to make flour, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kinds of things like supplied them with certain hardware and things. But he-- he was like a little broker. And-- and-- and when we were in the woods, I mean, when we were in the concentration camp, we had six or eight people said we're gonna escape. Then-- then something leaked out. Yeah, and another thing, we had-- we had wearing suits, like a coat, with like crosses, to recognize like we, like Jew refugees. But one thing, we prepared ourselves. I-- I bought myself a coat, because this was summertime. A jacket. And my brother, to be safe, we hide-- we hide this in the barracks, and shoes. Because we had shoes.  Not regular shoes, just [INAUDIBLE] as they called it. Special shoes that everybody recognized as like if you were in jail, special [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suits, special jackets to wear. Then we did this, and my brother-- I bought myself a pair of shoes, and my brother. The last minute, last night before we escape-- we wanted to escape, the [? officer-- ?] the manager from the barrack came over, and he said, [INAUDIBLE]. Give me your shoes. Somebody leaked out that we-- that we want to escape, and took away the shoes from him. Just leave, but we were prepared. We had-- we had another another pair of shoes in another room. This-- this pair of shoes, they didn't find. But anyway, we-- when we went to work, when we went to work the next morning, we said we're going to escape. We couldn't do it. It didn't work. They wanted to escape. They couldn't. Then we didn't do it. And it took us maybe about a few days. We-- we changed the whole situation. And I brought like a pair of pliers, which to cut electric wires, because I used to work in that-- in that shop, which had all kinds of tools. And I opened-- I took away a piece of wood from the toilet, made it loose, just hanging. And at night, 7, 8 o'clock at night was dark and we escaped. We escaped that time maybe about 40 fellows of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: 40. I didn't know about the 40. I only knew about eight. But the-- the other-- evidently, the others organized themselves, too. Because we knew it's getting closer that the Russians are gonna start an offensive. This was-- we didn't know exactly when this will happen, but we-- we heard rumors that they're gonna-- it's going to be a change. And I escaped [? that ?] [? night. ?] That Night I escaped with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you do this escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. The escape was like this. As I told you, I cut those wires, the electric wires. This was maybe about a day before. When I cut the electric wires a day before, and I took away one piece of wood from the-- from the wall, it made it loose. Only that I should be able just to-- to sneak into, to go through. Nothing was noticed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were eight of us like this. When I escaped, happened in my place, when I escaped, I fell in a little hole. And maybe five minutes later, the hole, we were-- the-- the-- they watched us from the top. How do you call this? A special station. They had the electric station to-- to watch us. And-- and I fell in there, and I couldn't nothing. I couldn't get out and I couldn't go in. I was stuck in a little hole. This-- and this and this place saved me. When I was there, it was already quiet, maybe two hours. I said, how do I get out? What I did, I found a piece of wood. I broke it, because at night, you hear any little touch.  You hear it. Slowly, I break it up, and I put it in the-- in the-- on the-- on-- in the [INAUDIBLE], and I made like a little [INAUDIBLE]. And I-- and I 1, 2, 3, and I go-- and I went-- and I walked out. When I was already on the-- on the-- how do you call it? [LAUGHS] When I came out on the-- on the ground, yes. I couldn't pick myself up. I walked on my stomach on, on, on. Maybe 30 feet was another guy holding up my friend. He said-- I asked him the first question, did you see my brother? He lost his brother, and I lost my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were walking. We was on our stomachs maybe about another 30, 40 feet. And we picked ourselves up, and we looked. Where we looked, and-- and at night, you hear any little-- any little move, you hear. Then we heard that somebody's talking to each other. Then I knew right away somebody is from our fellows.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then slowly, slowly I walked over to them. It was another two-- two fellows of ours. They asked me, where-- where do we go? I was not too far away from-- we used to live, let's say, maybe like 10 miles from this area where we were-- where I was in-- in the barrack. And I said, look. Now we're going to go. I headed in that direction. Let's go this in this direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We walked over. And I-- yeah, during this time, I stole a lot of things, and I gave everything to a Polack, that he should prepare for us a place to hide. He-- he took from us-- he took from me a lot of money, and a lot of goods, and he was supposed to prepare us. And I worked, and I went in this same night. We walked over that fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came over to this fellow, to the house, the fellow was not home. The wife is home. I say, where's Mr. Sarzsinski? She said, no, he went to work. I knew it was a trick. He didn't went to work. I say, look. We are not alone. We have-- we-- we are here maybe a hundred people. If you don't tell me where your husband is, we're gonna burn your house. And she was making bread. Like she was-- she-- she-- how do you call it? She made bread. She made bread for herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, look, my husband is not here. If you want to stay over here, stay over and tomorrow morning he'll be back. Then we were-- then we were afraid to be in the house. Then we walked out from the house, and we went to the woods. It wasn't too far away, the woods. And on the-- on the-- going away, we saw the Germans, the soldiers. So many soldiers on the-- Maybe 50 feet away was the station. And we stayed till-- till like 4 or 5 o'clock in the morning, and we went back to him, because I wanted to see him. I wanted to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you? How many men were with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were at that time, we were four people. Me and my-- me and-- four fellows. We were four fellows there. [DRINKS] When we were four fellows over there, I-- we went back to her. And she-- and he said-- she said to me, he's still not here. He still-- he didn't come back. Then I said to the fellows, I said, look, we cannot be here because we're gonna get hurt. Then we walked away from this place. And we were hanging around another day in this-- this place. We took one bread from that woman, and we-- we slept for [INAUDIBLE] another day. Not a day, I mean, until night. At night time, we went back to that-- to that-- to that girl, to that Sarzsinski, and he was home. He was home. I say, Mr. Sarzsinski, you promised us a hiding place. He said, sure, I have it. You're gonna go with me, and you're going to have this place. But now, he said, I can't go away. Tomorrow morning I'll meet you at this and this place. Don't stay here. And we were afraid to stay here. We walked away from him. And we worked-- we went to the same woods where we were, during the day, hanging around. And we were waiting. He said he's gonna be 6 o'clock, 7 o'clock. He didn't show up.
